text: Breakthroughs in fusion have the potential to help us produce the clean energy we need by 2050. One of the great things about this energy source is that once it’s fully commercialized, it can be built anywhere and scaled up—with no carbon emissions and minimal waste. Here are a few other promising technologies that could help us get to a zero-emission future:
Next-generation electricity is almost here | bill gates

This is huge news for the fight against tuberculosis. The world health organization is recommending near point-of-care tests that can use tongue swabs to collect samples and diagnose TB within an hour—making testing faster, simpler, and more accessible.​
​
tools like this will reach more people, catch TB earlier, and start treatment sooner. Grateful to the scientists and partners who made this progress possible.

New tongue-swab TB test could help eradicate the disease, WHO says
The breakthroughs happening in geothermal, fusion, and geologic hydrogen have the potential to help make clean electricity widely available to more people.

Reflecting on my time at the breakthrough energy ventures investor summit last week, I’m left incredibly energized.
The pace of progress is exceeding even my most optimistic expectations. It’s clear that real breakthroughs are happening, and they’re happening faster and more effectively than I imagined.
What’s most exciting is the potential impact: these advances will help make reliable energy more accessible to more people around the world. That kind of momentum is inspiring to be part of.

Well-lit rooms, reliable power, and cold storage for medicine are things many of us don’t think twice about—but in rural health facilities across uganda, they’re far from guaranteed. Programs like this, which ensure access to reliable energy, can make a huge difference in people’s lives.
How solar is powering maternal care and immunisation in rural uganda

For nearly 40 years, pharmaceutical drug donations have played a transformative role in combating neglected tropical diseases. These programs deliver medicine at scale, making it possible for countries’ public health systems to prevent and treat NTDs at no cost to patients. The collaboration and generosity that enable these donations are critical to eliminating NTDs for good—and one of global health’s greatest success stories.
Bonus: ‘the threshold’ live on world neglected tropical disease day
Saidi jafari lost his wife and daughter to childbirth complications. Today, he’s an m-mama driver—doing his part to make sure that no other family has to suffer that same fate.
Media player modal window

Geothermal has the potential to be one of our most reliable and scalable sources of clean energy, and the expertise to unlock it already exists.
By applying the experience of oil and gas engineers, drillers, and scientists, companies like fervo energy are shaping the next generation of clean energy and demonstrating how an industry fueled by innovation can deliver dependable power while creating high-quality jobs for a skilled workforce.

The future of geothermal energy may depend on fossil fuel workers

The best hospital in the world could be just ten miles away. But if a pregnant woman in crisis can’t reach it, it might as well not exist for her. ​​
​​
that’s why m-mama created a kind of 911 for rural africa—coordinating cars, boats, motorbikes, and community drivers to get women in labor to the care they need when every minute counts.
With m-mama, phone calls save lives | bill gates

India’s biological e achieved a major milestone and is now manufacturing a lifesaving polio vaccine that will be critical to stopping certain outbreaks of the virus. It’s just one more example of why, despite the setbacks we saw last year, global health progress is a bell that can’t be unrung.

A decisive step forward in stopping polio outbreaks — at speed and at scale.
Biological e. Limited has won phase II pre-qualification from the world health organization for nopv2, validating fully integrated manufacturing from drug substance to finished vaccine — all at one site.
700m+ doses already powering global response. Now with stronger, end-to-end who-qualified capacity when the world needs it most.
Link to the full article is in the comments. #nopv2 #polio #polioeradication #who #prequalification #biologicale #be

I’m delighted to begin the year by sharing several appointments that strengthen the gates foundation’s efforts to improve lives. Please join me in recognizing these leaders in their new roles.
First, we welcome dr. Sri mulyani indrawati to our governing board. Sri brings deep global and regional experience from her leadership at the world bank group and the international monetary fund, as well as her service as indonesia’s longest-serving and first female minister of finance, including through the difficult days of the covid-19 pandemic. She adds fresh perspective for the board as it guides the foundation’s direction over the next 20 years.
I’m also pleased to announce two leadership appointments that build on deep, long-standing contributions from within the foundation.
Hari menon will serve as president of our global growth and opportunity division. Over nearly two decades at the foundation, hari led our gates foundation india office and work across south and south-east asia. In this new role, he will help integrate and advance our efforts to expand inclusive growth and economic opportunity globally.
Ankur vora becomes president of our newly-created africa & india offices division, alongside his role as chief strategy officer. Ankur has been a force in shaping our strategy and strengthening the execution of programs around the world. This new division reflects our commitment to elevating country and regional voices, ensuring strategies are informed by lived experience and deliver measurable impact.
At a moment of both urgency and opportunity across global health and development, these leaders strengthen our connection to local work and the voices driving progress in their communities.
I’m grateful to sri, hari, and ankur for stepping into these roles, and I look forward to working together as we continue advancing the foundation’s mission.

I’m hopeful that the new year means new solutions—and renewed momentum—in the fight to save and improve lives around the world.

I have always been an optimist. These days, though, my optimism comes with footnotes. For the first time this century, the world slipped backwards on key measures like childhood mortality in 2025, and if we don’t make the right decisions, this trend will continue. At the same time, I also believe that, within the next decade, the world will not only get back on track but enter a new era of unprecedented progress. As we start 2026, I’m thinking about how the year ahead will position us for decades to come.

Friends and colleagues often ask me how I stay optimistic during hard times. Lately, that has become a difficult question to answer. The truth is, I am still an optimist. But these days, my optimism comes with footnotes. As we head into 2026, I’m thinking about how the year ahead will set us up for the decades to come:

Losing my dad to this disease makes the fight for alzheimer’s breakthroughs very personal to me. Recent advancements in alzheimer’s diagnostics specifically, like blood-based tests and digital biomarkers, help me stay optimistic about the future.
A new era for alzheimer's disease diagnosis | think global health

I read a lot throughout the year, and pulling together my annual list of recommendations has become one of my favorite traditions. These are five books I especially enjoyed in 2025. ​
​
remarkably bright creatures by shelby van pelt: a terrific novel about a woman who finds fulfillment befriending a clever octopus. ​
​
who knew by barry diller: a fascinating memoir full of insights from a transformative career that reshaped the entertainment industry. ​
​
abundance by ezra klein & derek thompson: a timely explanation of why it’s so hard to build in america today and what it will take to change that. ​
​
clearing the air by hannah ritchie: a clear, data-driven look at what really matters in the fight against climate change, without doom or denial. ​
​
when everyone know that everyone knows by steven pinker: a thought-provoking exploration of how our understanding of what others know drives trust, cooperation, and progress.

Krystal birungi is living proof that global health programs matter. Growing up in uganda, she saw malaria devastate her community. But the global fund made treatment and diagnosis possible, drastically reducing malaria deaths over the course of less than two decades. Now, krystal works with target malaria at the uganda virus research institute as an entomologist, exploring new malaria-fighting innovations so that children don’t suffer as she did in 1995.
Her efforts—and those of global health programs—are working. When krystal was growing up, one in three ugandan children didn’t live past their fifth birthday. Thanks to global health progress, that number is now one in 25—but that positive trend is changing.
The health funding cuts we’ve seen over the past year to programs like the one that saved krystal’s life affect real people—and continued cuts will mean a reversal of progress. In fact, they could lead to 12-16 million more children dying over the next 20 years from causes we know how to prevent. Yes, these numbers are alarming. But they are not inevitable. We have a chance to change course—to come together as a global community and save millions of children’s lives so they can live past their fifth birthdays and have the opportunity to accomplish their dreams.
Our goal is for no children to die from preventable diseases. We can’t stop at almost.
I was fortunate to spend time with krystal at our new york goalkeepers event in september and learn how her work as an entomologist is bringing us closer to a future where no child dies from this preventable disease. Her contributions to science and public health, and the story she shares so generously with us here, are invaluable as we continue to fight malaria.
Hear more from krystal about the tools and innovations that can help us reach full elimination of malaria, and read our 2025 gates foundation goalkeepers report—including an essay from krystal—out today: #goalkeepers2030

Over the past several months, the gates foundation has worked with the institute for health metrics and evaluation (ihme) to quantify the impact of health funding cuts. What we found was sobering. But it’s not irreversible. Here’s how we can leverage smart spending and innovation at scale to save millions of children’s lives:

I don’t read much fiction, but remarkably bright creatures won me over. The novel follows the relationship of a 70-year-old aquarium janitor named tova with a giant pacific octopus named marcellus—and offers a thoughtful reflection on the meaning of life and growing older:
These are five great books I couldn’t put down this year.
